1: 'If a man marries a woman and she finds no favor with him because he discovers some indecency in her, he shall write her a certificate of divorce' — the divorce law of Deuteronomy 24:1-4 is a case law, not a permission for divorce. Its legal focus is the re-marriage prohibition (Deuteronomy 24:4), not the regulation of divorce grounds. 'Some indecency' (ʿerwat dābār, literally 'nakedness of a thing') became the subject of the famous Shammai-Hillel dispute (Mishnah Gittin Deuteronomy 9:10): Shammai restricted it to sexual misconduct; Hillel extended it to anything displeasing the husband. Jesus addressed this dispute directly in Matthew 19:3-9, citing this verse.
2: 'Once she has left his house, she is free to marry another man' — the divorce certificate (sēfer kĕrîtût, document of cutting off) served a protective function for the divorced woman: it proved she was free to remarry and was not subject to adultery charges for a subsequent marriage. Without the certificate, a woman separated from her husband was in legal limbo — unable to remarry and unable to return. The certificate converted social abandonment into a legally recognized status change. Papyrus divorce documents from the ANE (and later Jewish practice) confirm the certificate's legal function.
3: 'But if her second husband also turns against her and divorces her, or if the second husband dies' — the law addresses a specific scenario: a twice-married woman seeking return to her first husband. The possibility of sequential marriages creates a complex legal situation that Israelite law felt necessary to regulate — the remarried divorced woman's status vis-à-vis her first husband needed clarification, especially since the first husband might claim remarriage with her as a return to the original covenant rather than a new marriage.
4: 'Her first husband may not take her back as his wife after she has been with another man — that is detestable before the LORD' — the prohibition protects the woman's dignity (she is not property to be disposed of and retrieved), prevents a circuit of serial divorce-and-remarriage for economic motives, and maintains the permanence principle of marriage despite the divorce concession. Jeremiah 3:1 uses this very law as an analogy for Israel's relationship with YHWH — Israel has played the harlot with other gods; yet YHWH extends an extraordinary offer to return, which is more remarkable precisely because the law would prohibit it for any human husband.
5: 'When a man has recently married, he shall not serve in the army or be required for any public service. He shall be free at home for one year' — the newlywed deferment from military service is extraordinary in the ANE context. Egyptian military records and Hittite vassal obligation documents do not include such provisions. Deuteronomy's deferment recognizes that establishing a household — the foundational social and economic unit — deserves protected time. 'To bring happiness to the wife he has married' (lĕśammēaḥ ʾet-ʾišĕtô) is remarkably explicit about the husband's social-emotional obligation to his new wife.
6: 'Do not take a mill or a millstone as collateral for a loan — that would be seizing someone's livelihood as a pledge' — the millstone was the household's most essential food-processing tool. Without it, a family could not grind grain into flour and therefore could not eat. Taking millstones as collateral (even legitimately held) was technically legal debt-recovery that amounted to functional starvation. Deuteronomy's prohibition against this category of pledge — 'that is taking a life as a pledge' (kî-nep̄eš hûʾ ḥōbēl) — grounds the economic law in the protection of human life.
7: 'If someone is caught kidnapping a fellow Israelite — treating him as a slave or selling him — that thief shall be put to death' — the law against man-stealing parallels the same protection in Exodus 21:16. In the ANE, kidnapping for sale into foreign slavery was a real commercial crime (Joseph: Genesis 37:27-28). The Code of Hammurabi (§14) prescribes death for kidnapping another man's minor son. Deuteronomy's provision protects adult free Israelites from reduction to slave status through the crime of abduction — 'purge the evil from your midst' is the covenant-purity formula applied to this economic crime.
8: 'In cases of skin disease, be very careful to follow exactly what the Levitical priests direct you' — the ṣāraʿat (skin disease, traditionally rendered 'leprosy' but covering various skin conditions) protocol belonged to the priestly domain (Lev 13-14). The civil authorities (Deuteronomy's frame) defer to the priestly experts for diagnosis and restoration. The public health dimension is significant: ṣāraʿat cases were excluded from the community until priestly examination and purification, preventing communicable disease spread in the densely settled town environment.
9: 'Remember what the LORD your God did to Miriam on the journey out of Egypt' — the Miriam narrative (Numbers 12:1-15) is cited as a warning against disregarding skin-disease protocol. Miriam's ṣāraʿat came as divine judgment for challenging Moses's authority; her seven-day exclusion from the camp was the standard protocol even when the afflicted person was the sister of the covenant mediator. The appeal to Miriam makes the point that no one — regardless of their social status or family connection — is exempt from the purity protocols.
10: 'When you make a loan to your neighbor, do not go into his home to take his pledge' — the pledge-taking law protects the debtor's domestic space. Entering a person's house to seize a pledge for a debt was a form of creditor intimidation that violated the household's sacred boundary. In ANE societies, the household was a legally and socially protected space; unauthorized entry — even by a creditor with a legitimate claim — was a trespass against the household's integrity. The law makes the debtor responsible for bringing the pledge outside, preserving his domestic dignity.
11: 'Stand outside and let him bring you his pledge' — the spatial protocol — creditor outside, debtor inside — preserves the legal and social boundary of the household. The creditor's power (the debt) cannot be exercised through physical domination of the debtor's space. This is a remarkable consumer-protection provision: the creditor has an enforceable claim but must exercise it through socially appropriate channels rather than through economic intimidation.
12: 'If he is poor, do not keep his pledge overnight' — the overnight-pledge restriction for the poor debtor. The pledge was typically something the debtor would need at night — the cloak that served as a blanket (Deuteronomy 24:13). An overnight pledge of a poor person's cloak was functional cruelty: the creditor held the poor man's warmth as security. The law requires daily return, making the pledge system available to both parties without turning economic security into nighttime suffering for the debtor.
13: 'Return his cloak at sunset so he can sleep warmly. He will bless you, and it will count as righteousness before the LORD your God' — the specific item (cloak/śalmāh) confirms this is a nighttime covering law. The creditor's righteousness (ṣĕdāqāh) before YHWH is credited by the act of returning the cloak — economic obligation is framed as covenant faithfulness. The 'he will bless you' adds the dimension of human gratitude: the poor man's blessing of his creditor creates relational goodwill that transcends the commercial transaction.
14: 'Do not mistreat a poor and needy hired worker — whether he is a fellow Israelite or a foreigner' — the śākîr (hired worker, day laborer) was the most vulnerable category of free worker: no land, no household support, dependent on daily wages for food. Day laborers appear in Jesus's parables (Matthew 20:1-16) as a recognizable social category still operating under similar economic conditions. The explicit inclusion of foreigners extends the protection beyond ethnic-covenant boundaries to any person in economic vulnerability within Israel's territory.
15: 'Pay his wages the same day, before sunset — he is poor and counting on it' — the same-day payment law reflects the reality of subsistence wages: the day laborer's family ate based on that day's wages. Withholding wages overnight meant the laborer's family went hungry that night. 'He is poor and counting on it' (kî ʿānî hûʾ ûʾēlāyw hûʾ nōśēʾ ʾet-nap̄šô: he is poor and sets his life on it) is a remarkable motivational clause — the law appeals to the worker's existential dependence rather than merely to legal obligation. Amos 8:4-6 and Malachi 3:5 condemn violations of exactly this wage-payment practice.
16: 'Parents shall not be put to death for the sins of their children, and children shall not be put to death for the sins of their parents. Each person shall be executed only for his own sin' — individual criminal responsibility is a significant legal principle in the ancient Near East, where collective family punishment was standard. The Code of Hammurabi (§230) required the builder's son to be killed if a house collapse killed the owner's son — a trans-generational collective penalty. Deuteronomy's individual-responsibility principle limits this: criminal execution punishes the individual offender only. This is distinct from the covenantal consequences that can run to generations (Deuteronomy 5:9) — legal execution and covenantal consequence operate differently.
17: 'Do not deprive the foreigner or the fatherless of justice, or take a widow's garment as collateral' — the triad of vulnerable people (foreigner, fatherless, widow) appears repeatedly in Deuteronomy (Deuteronomy 10:18; Deuteronomy 14:29; Deuteronomy 16:11,14; Deuteronomy 24:17-21; Deuteronomy 26:12-13; Deuteronomy 27:19). These three categories share the structural feature of having no male patron or advocate: the foreigner lacks tribal membership, the fatherless lacks a father's legal advocacy, the widow lacks a husband's economic protection. Deuteronomy consistently builds their protection into the legal system because the normal patron-client structures exclude them.
18: 'Remember that you were a slave in Egypt and the LORD your God redeemed you' — the exodus-memory motivation for protecting the vulnerable. The connection is direct: Israel was once the foreigner, the economically exploited, the one without legal advocate — and YHWH intervened for them. The covenant obligation to protect the foreigner, fatherless, and widow is grounded in Israel's own remembered experience of vulnerability, not merely in abstract ethical principle. This motivation appears seven times in Deuteronomy (Deuteronomy 5:15; Deuteronomy 15:15; Deuteronomy 16:12; Deuteronomy 24:18,22; cf. Exodus 22:21; Deuteronomy 23:9) — the most repeated ethical grounding in the book.
19: 'When you harvest your field and leave a sheaf behind, do not go back for it — leave it for the foreigner, the fatherless, and the widow' — the forgotten-sheaf law is the field equivalent of the vineyard-gleaning right of Deuteronomy 23:24-25. The provision is for the accidentally forgotten sheaf — not a required set-aside but a prohibition against retrieval of accidentally forgotten grain. This preserves the gleaner's claim to incidental leftovers without requiring the farmer to deliberately reduce their harvest. Ruth's gleaning in Boaz's field (Ruth 2:2-3) operates precisely within this legal framework.
20: 'When you have beaten the olives off your trees, do not go back for what is left' — the olive-beating law extends the gleaning provision to orchard crops. Olive trees were beaten with poles to dislodge ripe fruit; a second beating would recover additional olives but was forbidden. The economics are calibrated: the harvest is protected by its primary collection; the remnant — which requires additional labor — belongs to the poor by covenant law rather than to the owner's maximizing interest. The law creates a structural poverty-relief mechanism within normal agricultural operations.
21: 'When you harvest your vineyard, do not go back for every last bunch' — the grape-gleaning law (cf. Leviticus 19:9-10: 'do not glean your vineyard bare, do not pick up the fallen grapes; leave them for the poor and the foreigner'). The repetition across grain, olives, and grapes covers the three main crop categories of Israelite agriculture and the three main harvest times of the agricultural year (grain: spring; olives: autumn; grapes: late summer). The triple coverage is not redundant — it is systematic, ensuring that no crop type escapes the gleaning obligation.
22: 'Remember that you were a slave in Egypt. That is why I am giving you this command' — the exodus-memory motivation closes the gleaning section as it closed the protection-of-the-vulnerable section (Deuteronomy 24:18). The structural repetition underlines the connection: Israel's agricultural generosity toward the poor is rooted in YHWH's past generosity toward enslaved Israel. The covenant is a moral chain: YHWH's grace → Israel's gratitude → neighbor's benefit. The laws are not merely social welfare regulations but enacted covenant theology.
